behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces a route no more than 1.5x optimal for up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is purely algorithmic — the heuristic function either finds a good-enough route or it doesn't. No module boundary is crossed; the function takes coordinates and returns an ordered route. REAL-INFRA: None needed. MOCK-HIDE: No dependencies to mock — coordinates are pure data. LLM-DEP: No. The test needs a set of known coordinates with a computable optimal route length (e.g., small grid) and compares the heuristic output against the 1.5x bound.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a fixed set of ≤20 coordinate pairs for which the optimal route length is known (e.g., a convex polygon or a brute-force-computed baseline). Call the TSP heuristic function directly with those coordinates. Assert that the returned route's total Euclidean distance is ≤ 1.5× the optimal. Also verify the returned route visits every stop exactly once.

behaviors.description: Delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from distance and configured average speed per road type
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is arithmetic — time = distance / speed. The failure mode lives entirely within a single calculation function. There is no module boundary: the function receives distance and a road-type-keyed speed config and returns time windows. REAL-INFRA: None. MOCK-HIDE: No dependency to mock; speed config is a plain dict. LLM-DEP: No. A unit test with fixed inputs fully covers this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call the time-window estimation function directly with a table of (distance, road_type, speed_config) inputs and assert the returned window matches the expected value (distance / speed). Cover multiple road types (e.g., highway vs. urban) to verify the config lookup is correct. Include an edge case where distance is 0 (stop at the same location).

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The validator receives a total-estimated-time value and a remaining-shift-hours value and makes a binary accept/reject decision while computing excess. This is a single function's logic — no handoff to another module is involved in the failure mode. REAL-INFRA: None. MOCK-HIDE: No dependency exists to mock. LLM-DEP: No. A unit test drives all branches (exactly at limit, over limit, under limit).
behaviors.test_description: Call the shift validator function directly with (total_time, remaining_shift) pairs covering: total_time < remaining_shift (accepted, no excess), total_time == remaining_shift (boundary — accepted with 0 excess), and total_time > remaining_shift (rejected, excess = total_time - remaining_shift). Assert the return value for each case matches the expected outcome and excess amount.

behaviors.description: When one address fails geocoding, the service skips that stop, optimizes remaining addresses, and reports which addresses were skipped
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a cross-module handoff: the geocoding layer signals a no-results result, the orchestration layer must detect it, remove the stop from the candidate list before passing it to the optimizer, and accumulate the skipped address in a separate report. A unit test on any single module cannot catch a bug where, say, the failed address silently falls through to the optimizer or disappears from the skipped-addresses list. The failure only surfaces when the geocoding adapter, the filtering/orchestration logic, the optimizer input builder, and the skip-reporting path all interact. REAL-INFRA: Geocoding API is external HTTP, but it can be stubbed at the HTTP client or adapter layer. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the geocoding call at the right adapter boundary exposes the orchestration bug without hiding it. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the full service pipeline (geocoding adapter, optimizer, skip reporter) with the geocoding HTTP client replaced by a stub that returns a no-results response for exactly one address and valid coordinates for all others. Invoke the service with a list of N addresses (one of which is the failing one). Assert: (a) the optimizer is called with N-1 stops (not N), (b) the returned route contains no stop for the failing address, (c) the response's skipped-addresses field lists the failing address exactly once.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication and item-consolidation is a self-contained preprocessing step on the input list. The failure mode — producing two stops instead of one, or losing delivery items during merge — lives in the consolidation function itself. The optimizer is only ever handed the deduplicated list; if consolidation is correct, the optimizer visiting the stop once follows trivially. REAL-INFRA: None. MOCK-HIDE: No dependency to mock. LLM-DEP: No. A unit test on the consolidation function with a crafted input list fully covers this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Call the address-consolidation function with an input list containing two entries for the same address, each with distinct delivery items. Assert: (a) the output contains exactly one stop for that address, (b) the stop's delivery-items collection is the union of both input entries' items, (c) all other addresses in the list remain unaffected. Also test that three or more duplicates collapse to one stop with all items merged.
